folkarts.name: Therukoothu
folkarts.desc: Translating to "Street Theatre," this is a highly stylized, multi-disciplinary folk performance combining acting, singing, dance, and vibrant makeup.

frame1.modalContent: Performers wear elaborate wooden ornaments known as bujai kattai (shoulder pieces) and large, gilded headgear decorated with small mirrors.

folkarts.name: Parai Aattam
folkarts.desc: An ancient, vibrant Tamil folk art combining the rhythmic, energetic drumming of the Parai (a traditional frame drum made of wood and leather) with vigorous dancing

frame1.modalContent: With over 220 participants from West Spring Primary School, National Junior College, Temasek Polytechnic, and organizations like Omkar Arts and Damaru Singapore, the events included hands-on drumming sessions, dialogues, and Q&A interactions.

frame1.alt: An ancient folk dance and art form, pre-Sangam
frame1.modalTitle: An ancient folk dance and art form, pre-Sangam
frame1.modalContent: Historically, it served as a crucial communication tool for announcements, war, farming, and celebrations before being restricted to funerals due to caste-based stigma, but is now witnessing a resurgence as a symbol of cultural identity and resistance.

folkarts.name: Karagaattam
folkarts.desc: Karagaattam is a vibrant Tamil folk dance centred on balance, rhythm, and celebration. Dancers perform with a decorated karagam, or pot, balanced on the head, creating a form that is both visually striking and deeply rooted in community tradition. In ATAM’s Nam Marabu Series 2 (2024), Karagaattam was presented together with Thavil, highlighting the close relationship between movement and live percussion.

frame1.modalContent: Held from 8 to 11 May 2024, Nam Marabu Series 2 introduced Karagaattam and Thavil through an in-house dialogue session at the Indian Heritage Centre and outreach workshops across studios and schools in Singapore. The series brought the folk art closer to students, dance enthusiasts, and first-time learners.

frame2.modalContent: Held from 8 to 11 May 2024, Nam Marabu Series 2 introduced Karagaattam and Thavil through an in-house dialogue session at the Indian Heritage Centre and outreach workshops across studios and schools in Singapore. The series brought the folk art closer to students, dance enthusiasts, and first-time learners.

folkarts.name: Salangai Kittiyattam
folkarts.desc: Kittiyattam is a vibrant and rare Tamil folk dance tradition deeply rooted in the agrarian and temple festival culture of South India.

frame1.modalContent: Salangai Kittiyattam is a traditional South Indian folk practice where rhythm, movement and devotion come together through the sounding of the salangai — bells attached to the wooden stick.

folkarts.name: Tholpaavai Koothu
folkarts.desc: A form of shadow puppetry practiced in Kerala and Tamil Nadu

frame1.modalContent: The photo shows a scene from Tholpaavai Koothu performance.

frame2.modalContent: The photo shows a scene from Tholpaavai Koothu performance.

frame1.modalContent: Photo showing the image of Bhadrakali (Goddess in Hindu customs)

frame2.modalContent: Photo showing the image of Bhadrakali (Goddess in Hindu customs)

frame3.modalContent: Photo showing the image of Bhadrakali (Goddess in Hindu customs)

frame4.modalContent: Photo showing the image of Bhadrakali (Goddess in Hindu customs)

frame1.modalContent:
These are permanent high stages for the conduct of the show in all the Bhagvati temples. 

It is known as “Koothu Madam” meaning shadow play theatre. usually the length of “Koothu Madam” will be 42 feet and 8 feet in there will be curtain through out in front of the madam and the puppet show behind will conduct the shadow play with the help of burning oil wick lamps . 

Usually the curtain should face the deity of the temples. The upper portion of the curtain will be white in color and the lower part will be black. 

Puppets are held or mobilized between the lamps and the curtain so as to develop the sharp shadows on the curtain. The puppets are fixed on the screen.

frame2.modalContent:
On the wooden beam –vilaku madam – behind the curtain are placed 21 lighted lamps equidistant from each other. Husked coconuts cut in halves serve as lamps, half filled with coconut oil and lit with cotton wicks set in a row from one end of the stage to the other. They illuminate the screen uniformly.

In a few temples however, cressets –chiratu- made of clay have been used instead. Cresset lamps, with an upper and lower compartment, are specially made for Tholpaavai Koothu. The upper cresset is the lamp, oil is poured in to it and the wick is lighted. The lower compartment has holes where water is poured, preventing the clay lamp from getting over heated.

frame3.modalContent:
The chief accompaniments for Tholpaavai Koothu are drum - Ezhupara , Madahalam, Kuzhal, Ilathalam, Conch, Chilanka.

To add to the dramatic impart of certain scenes the arists produce special sound effects and also recite Vaitaris - rhythmic syllables. A fight scene is accented by cries of Sapai Sapai from behind the stage.

The performance is accompanied by the recitation of slokas and the performers are required to learn over 3000 of these before they perform. The recitation is accompanied by the musical instruments.

frame4.modalContent:
The chief accompaniments for Tholpaavai Koothu are drum - Ezhupara , Madahalam, Kuzhal, Ilathalam, Conch, Chilanka.

To add to the dramatic impart of certain scenes the arists produce special sound effects and also recite Vaitaris - rhythmic syllables. A fight scene is accented by cries of Sapai Sapai from behind the stage.

The performance is accompanied by the recitation of slokas and the performers are required to learn over 3000 of these before they perform. The recitation is accompanied by the musical instruments.

folkarts.name: Sakkai Kutchi Aattam
folkarts.desc: Sakkai Kuchi Atam or Sakkaiyattam is a traditional folk dance originating from Tamil Nadu. Traditionally it involves using the fingers to strike four linked pieces of teak wood to produce sound. Nowadays, as it's more widely practiced modern form, performers use both hands to strike bamboo poles—about two feet in length—to create rhythmic music for the dance.

folkarts.name: Oyilattam
folkarts.desc: Oyilattam is a traditional Tamil folk dance known for its grace, rhythmic footwork, and lively group movement. The word “oyil” is associated with beauty and grace, and the form is often performed with colourful handkerchiefs, ankle bells, and strong rhythmic accompaniment. In ATAM’s Nam Marabu Series 1 (2024), Oyilattam was introduced as one of the iconic Tamil folk dances taught through hands-on workshops for both newcomers and enthusiasts in Singapore.

frame1.modalContent: ATAM’s first Nam Marabu series introduced Indian traditional folk arts in Singapore through workshops and dialogue sessions led by Kalaimamani Ilangovan. Oyilattam was one of the featured forms shared with participants

The Rajakambalathu Nayakkar are a Telugu-origin community believed to have migrated from the Vijayanagar region of Andhra Pradesh and later settled in Tamil Nadu, where they have lived for generations.
Mr Nellai Manikandan is known for his expertise in Oyilattam, Thappattam, Karagam, and Silambam. He was also the master choreographer for Namma Ooru Thiruvizha - Chennai Sangamam in 2023 and 2024.

frame2.modalContent: Mr Nellai Manikandan is known for his expertise in Oyilattam, Thappattam, Karagam, and Silambam. He was also the master choreographer for Namma Ooru Thiruvizha - Chennai Sangamam in 2023 and 2024.

folkarts.name: Bihu
folkarts.desc: The Bihu dance is renowned for its energetic and lively movements, reflecting the exuberance of the Bohag Bihu festival that celebrates and welcomes spring and the life-giving spring rain.

frame1.modalContent: Through a series of workshops and community sessions, ATAM and Mr Gurunathan engaged over 160 participants across diverse age groups and backgrounds. His energetic and participatory teaching style encouraged dialogue around cultural identity and intergenerational transmission of knowledge.

frame2.modalContent: Through a series of workshops and community sessions, ATAM and Mr Gurunathan engaged over 160 participants across diverse age groups and backgrounds. His energetic and participatory teaching style encouraged dialogue around cultural identity and intergenerational transmission of knowledge.

frame1.modalContent: The earliest references to this traditional dance form can be traced back to the Ahom kingdom, where it was performed as a celebration of the arrival of spring. These historical accounts underscore the enduring cultural significance and heritage of Bihu dance within the Assamese community.

folkarts.name: Thudumbattam
folkarts.desc:
Thudumbattam is a vibrant tribal folk dance from Kerala, India, characterized by rhythmic drumming and synchronized group movements. It is both a musical and dance performance, where the beat of the “thudumbu” drum guides the dancers’ steps.

The art form emphasizes unity, energy, and cultural expression. It is typically performed in open spaces during festivals and communal gatherings, reflecting the traditions and lifestyle of indigenous communities.

frame1.modalContent: The dance is usually performed during festivals and social gatherings. It reflects community bonding and showcases the cultural identity of the people who perform it.

frame2.modalContent: The dance is usually performed during festivals and social gatherings. It reflects community bonding and showcases the cultural identity of the people who perform it.

frame1.modalContent: The dance was traditionally performed during harvest seasons, rituals, and important community events. The use of the “thudumbu” drum plays a key role in its origin and development.

frame1.modalContent: Through a series of workshops and community sessions, Mr Gurunathan engaged over 160 participants across diverse age groups and backgrounds. His energetic and participatory teaching style encouraged dialogue around cultural identity and intergenerational transmission of knowledge.

folkarts.name: Naattupura Paattu - Folk Singing
folkarts.desc: Tamil folk music, known as Naattupura Paattu, represents the indigenous melodic tradition of the rural Tamil landscape.

frame1.modalContent:
A graduate with a BA in Music, Ms. Suganthi rose to global prominence on 
Vijay TV’s Super Singer 7. She famously broke the stigma surrounding folk vocalists by recording for Maestro Ilaiyaraaja in Viduthalai Part 2, proving that folk nuances are as complex as any classical composition.

frame2.modalContent:

frame1.modalContent: Early Tamil literature, particularly the Ainkurunuru and Purananuru, contains numerous references to songs associated with specific topographies—such as the Kurinji (mountains) or Marutham (agricultural lands). These songs functioned as a vital oral archive, documenting local histories, social hierarchies, and dialects that were frequently omitted from court-sanctioned literature. Over centuries, these traditions evolved into distinct genres tailored to life transitions, ranging from the celebratory Thalattu (lullaby) to the somber Oppaari (funeral lament).

frame1.modalContent: This method prioritises raw emotional projection and volume over the refined, microtonal precision found in classical forms. The vocal style is designed to carry across vast open fields or through the din of a crowded festival.

frame1.modalContent: The late 20th century saw a resurgence in its popularity through the work of contemporary collectors and performers who documented thousands of traditional melodies to ensure their survival in the digital age. The influence of folk music is now pervasive in the Tamil film industry (Kollywood), where traditional rhythms and vocal styles are frequently hybridised with electronic and orchestral arrangements.

folkarts.name: Kalbeliya
folkarts.desc: The traditional dance of the Rajasthani nomadic snake-charmer tribe, Kalbeliya is inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity

frame1.modalContent: A premier practitioner of the Kalbeliya tradition, Ms. Chanana represents the nomadic heritage of Rajasthan. Her work focuses on preserving the authenticity of the serpentine movements and rhythmic agility that characterize this world-renowned folk form.

frame2.modalContent: A premier practitioner of the Kalbeliya tradition, Ms. Chanana represents the nomadic heritage of Rajasthan. Her work focuses on preserving the authenticity of the serpentine movements and rhythmic agility that characterize this world-renowned folk form.

frame1.modalContent:
The Kalbeliya dance, which is believed to have formed only in the 1980s, reflects their ancient occupation of snake handling and trading venom. Gulabo Sapera, a native of the Kalbeliya community, is credited with transforming and popularising the dance in the 1980s, bringing it to both national and international prominence - a contribution that earned her the Padma Shri in 2016. Women of the tribe would thereafter learn and 
perform Kalbeliya in a range of festivals and showcases.

frame1.modalContent: A traditional South Asian wind instrument, famously used by snake charmers in India and Pakistan. Crafted from a dried bottle gourd and two bamboo pipes.

frame2.modalContent: The Daf and Dholak are traditional South Asian percussion instruments, often used together in folk music, weddings, and devotional songs. The Daf is a large, single-headed frame drum of Persian origin. The Dholak is a two-headed, barrel-shaped drum played with both hands.

frame1.alt: Kalbelia  songs and dances of Rajasthan
frame1.modalTitle: Kalbelia  songs and dances of Rajasthan
frame1.modalContent: Today, women in flowing black skirts dance and swirl, replicating the movements of a serpent, while men accompany them on the khanjari percussion instrument and the poongi, a woodwind instrument traditionally played to capture snakes.
